title: Hyponatraemia — Workup
oneLiner: Serum Na <135 mmol/L; common in hospitalised pts; risks are cerebral oedema (low Na) and osmotic demyelination (over-correction) — classify by volume status.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Severe symptomatic hyponatraemia** (Na <120, seizures, coma, reduced GCS)  
  — *single finding*: new-onset seizure or GCS <13 without other cause → needs hypertonic saline *now*.
- **Adrenal crisis** (primary AI causing hyponatraemia + shock)  
  — *single finding*: hypotension not responding to fluids + hyperpigmentation (if chronic) → give IV hydrocortisone *before* waiting for cortisol result.
- **Pseudohyponatraemia** (normal measured osmolality, low lab Na)  
  — *single finding*: labs show low Na *but* serum osmolality ≥285 mOsm/kg → cause is hyperlipidaemia/paraproteinaemia; treat underlying, not the Na.
sections.Presentation:
- **Symptoms** (correlate with rate & depth):
  - Mild (Na 125–134): nausea, headache, lethargy
  - Moderate (Na 120–124): vomiting, confusion, gait disturbance
  - Severe (<120 or rapid drop): seizures, obtundation, respiratory arrest
- **Exam to classify volume status:**
  - **Hypovolaemic**: dry mucous membranes, low JVP, postural hypotension, reduced skin turgor
  - **Euvolaemic**: normal JVP, no oedema, no postural drop
  - **Hypervolaemic**: raised JVP, peripheral oedema, crackles (if heart failure)
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Pseudohyponatraemia** — NORMAL serum osmolality; check lipids, protein
- **Hypovolaemic hyponatraemia** (e.g. diarrhoea/vomiting, diuretics, Addison’s) — low volume + urine Na <20 (non-renal) vs >20 (renal)
- **Euvolaemic hyponatraemia** (SIADH, hypothyroidism, glucocorticoid deficiency, polydipsia) — normal volume + urine osmolality >100 (SIADH) / <100 (polydipsia)
- **Hypervolaemic** (heart failure, cirrhosis, CKD) — oedema + raised JVP + low urine Na (<20)
- **Splitter:** **Urine osmolality** <100 mOsm/kg → primary polydipsia or beer potomania (low solute intake); >100 mOsm/kg → SIADH or hypovolaemic/hypervolaemic (but need volume status + urine Na)
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside (first hour):**
  - Assess volume status (orthostatics if safe)
  - Urine dipstick (protein? glucose? to suggest CKD/diabetes)
  - **Capillary refill, JVP**
  - If severe symptoms: **3% NaCl ready** — do not delay bloods
- **Bloods:**
  - **U&E**, glucose (correct Na for hyperglycaemia: Na + 1.6 per 5 mmol/L glucose >5)
  - **Serum osmolality** (essential — distinguishes true from pseudohyponatraemia)
  - **Cortisol (random)** If suspected adrenal insufficiency: cortisol <100 nmol/L strongly suggestive; start hydrocortisone empirically while waiting.
  - **TSH, free T4** (hypothyroidism)
  - **Lipid profile** (pseudohyponatraemia from hypertriglyceridaemia)
  - **Uric acid** (low in SIADH, high in hypovolaemia)
- **Imaging:**  
  - First-line: **CXR** (if suspect SIADH/chest pathology e.g. small cell lung cancer)
  - Second-line: CT head (if neurological signs refractory to correction)
- **Special tests:**
  - **Urine osmolality + urine Na** (paired with serum) — key to classification.
  - **Short synacthen test** (if random cortisol borderline 100–400 nmol/L)
- **Diagnostic criteria:**  
  - **SIADH criteria** (Bartter & Schwartz):
    - Low plasma osmolality (<275 mOsm/kg)
    - Inappropriately concentrated urine (urine osmolality >100)
    - High urine Na (>20–40 mmol/L, with normal salt/water intake)
    - Clinical euvolaemia
    - No renal/thyroid/adrenal/glucocorticoid deficiency
- **Never-miss test:** **Random cortisol** (or 09:00 cortisol) to screen for adrenal insufficiency — omission can lead to fatal shock if steroids not given.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - **Severe symptoms** (seizures/coma/decreased GCS):
    - Start **3% sodium chloride** (do not wait for full labs) — typical: 150 mL IV over 15–20 min ([check local protocol]).
    - Aim for **5 mmol/L rise in 1 hour** (or until symptoms resolve).
  - **Hypotensive / adrenal crisis suspected**:
    - Give **hydrocortisone 100 mg IV stat** (after blood for cortisol).
    - 0.9% saline 500 mL bolus (if hypovolaemic) — but avoid in SIADH.
  - **Mild–moderate** (Na >120, no severe symptoms):
    - Stop any offending drugs (thiazides, SSRIs, carbamazepine, etc.)
    - Identify volume status → guide fluid strategy.
- **Ward (first 24–72 hours):**
  - **Hypovolaemic**: replace with **0.9% saline** (1–2 L/day depending on deficit); monitor Na q6–8h.
  - **Euvolaemic (SIADH)**:
    - **Fluid restrict** to 800–1000 mL/day (or less if Na <125).
    - Avoid 0.9% saline (will worsen hyponatraemia).
  - **Hypervolaemic**: treat underlying (e.g. heart failure — diuretics, no further saline).
  - **Monitor Na** every 6–8 hours (more frequently if correcting rapidly).
  - **Target correction rate**:
    - Chronic hyponatraemia (>48h duration or unknown): **≤10 mmol/L in 24 hours** (≤8 in high-risk groups — alcoholism, malnourished, liver disease).
    - Acute (<48h): can correct more aggressively (up to 1–2 mmol/L/h) to prevent cerebral oedema.
  - **If overcorrection** (rise >10 mmol/L in 24h):
    - Stop hypertonic saline, give **desmopressin 1–2 µg IV/SC** to re-lower Na, and/or give D5% IV (1000 mL over 3–4 h) — discuss with senior.
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Address cause (stop causative drug, treat SIADH with fluid restriction ± urea 15–30 g daily if NICE-converted, or tolvaptan under specialist guidance — [check local policy]).
  - **Adrenal insufficiency**: lifelong hydrocortisone + fludrocortisone.
  - **Hypothyroidism**: levothyroxine.
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - **Fluids**: either restricted (SIADH) or 0.9% saline (hypovolaemic) — *never* 0.9% saline in euvolaemic SIADH.
  - **VTE prophylaxis**: LMWH unless contraindicated.
  - **Diet**: NBM only if starting 3% NaCl (airway risk); otherwise normal diet.
  - **Observations**: NEWS2 + strict **intake/output chart** + daily weight.
  - **Daily U&Es**.
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **3% sodium chloride** (hypertonic saline):  
  - 150 mL over 15–20 min if severe symptoms — [check local protocol for exact rate].
- **Hydrocortisone** (adrenal crisis):  
  - 100 mg IV stat, then 50–100 mg IV q6h (or 200 mg/24h infusion).
  - *Check BNF or local guideline.*
- **Desmopressin** (DDAVP) for overcorrection:  
  - 1–2 µg IV/SC once — [check local policy].
- **Fluid restriction**: 800–1000 mL total/day for SIADH (adjust based on Na).
- **Key contraindications / interactions**:  
  - No thiazide diuretics in hyponatraemia (avoid).
  - NSAIDs worsen SIADH.
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment**:  
  - Tolvaptan contraindicated in liver disease (risk of hepatotoxicity).  
  - Fluid restriction in CKD may cause AKI — monitor creatinine.
- **Most dangerous dose error**:  
  - **Giving 0.9% saline to a euvolemic SIADH patient** → worsens hyponatraemia (water retention > Na gain).  
  - **Correcting chronic hyponatraemia too fast** (>10 mmol/L in 24h) → osmotic demyelination syndrome (ODS) — catastrophic.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute**:  
  - **Cerebral oedema** (if Na <120 rapid) → herniation, death.  
  - **Seizures** (from low Na).  
  - **Overcorrection** → **osmotic demyelination syndrome** (delayed 3–7 days): dysarthria, dysphagia, quadriparesis, locked-in syndrome.  
  - **Adrenal crisis** (if missed cause).
- **Chronic**:  
  - Increased falls/fractures (mild chronic hyponatraemia impairs gait).  
  - Osteoporosis (SIADH-related).  
  - Persistent symptoms (fatigue, cognitive impairment).
sections.Prognosis:
- Overall mortality depends on underlying cause (sepsis > SIADH > polydipsia).  
- Severe hyponatraemia (Na <120) with correct management: ~10% in-hospital mortality.  
- ODS is permanent if not recognised early.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Always measure paired serum + urine osmolality and urine Na** before starting treatment — avoids incorrect classification.  
- **If severe symptoms, start 3% NaCl immediately** — do not wait for osmolality (draw blood simultaneously).  
- **Stop all non-essential fluids** — often the cause is iatrogenic (e.g. 5% dextrose post-op).
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Do not give 0.9% saline for SIADH** — it's the wrong fluid (water retention > Na gain).  
- **Do not give D5% for hyponatraemia** — this is water, not Na.  
- **Do not correct chronic hyponatraemia too fast** — limit to ≤8 mmol/L in 24h if high risk (alcoholics, malnourished).  
- **Do not forget to check drug chart** — thiazides, SSRIs, carbamazepine, PPIs common culprits.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:**  
  - Worsening headache, confusion, vomiting, seizure.  
  - Inability to tolerate oral intake (risk of recurrence).  
  - Lower limb weakness or difficulty speaking (suggesting ODS — urgent re-admission).
- **Follow-up:**  
  - Repeat U&E in 1 week (or sooner if on new drugs).  
  - If SIADH: clinic review (renal/endocrine) to arrange long-term strategy.
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior (Reg/Consultant) immediately if:**  
  - Na <120 mmol/L (especially if symptomatic).  
  - Seizures / GCS <13.  
  - ODS suspected (new dysarthria/dysphagia after correction).  
  - Overcorrection >10 mmol/L in 24 h.  
  - Haemodynamic instability (likely adrenal crisis — need ITU/HDU).  
- **ICU referral:**  
  - Persistent seizures despite 3% NaCl.  
  - Need for continuous monitoring of Na correction (e.g. hourly).  
  - Adrenal crisis requiring inotropes.  
  - NEWS2 ≥7 or lactate >4 mmol/L.

rawMarkdown:
## Hyponatraemia — Workup
**One-liner:** Serum Na <135 mmol/L; common in hospitalised pts; risks are cerebral oedema (low Na) and osmotic demyelination (over-correction) — classify by volume status.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Severe symptomatic hyponatraemia** (Na <120, seizures, coma, reduced GCS)  
  — *single finding*: new-onset seizure or GCS <13 without other cause → needs hypertonic saline *now*.
- **Adrenal crisis** (primary AI causing hyponatraemia + shock)  
  — *single finding*: hypotension not responding to fluids + hyperpigmentation (if chronic) → give IV hydrocortisone *before* waiting for cortisol result.
- **Pseudohyponatraemia** (normal measured osmolality, low lab Na)  
  — *single finding*: labs show low Na *but* serum osmolality ≥285 mOsm/kg → cause is hyperlipidaemia/paraproteinaemia; treat underlying, not the Na.

### Presentation
- **Symptoms** (correlate with rate & depth):
  - Mild (Na 125–134): nausea, headache, lethargy
  - Moderate (Na 120–124): vomiting, confusion, gait disturbance
  - Severe (<120 or rapid drop): seizures, obtundation, respiratory arrest
- **Exam to classify volume status:**
  - **Hypovolaemic**: dry mucous membranes, low JVP, postural hypotension, reduced skin turgor
  - **Euvolaemic**: normal JVP, no oedema, no postural drop
  - **Hypervolaemic**: raised JVP, peripheral oedema, crackles (if heart failure)

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Pseudohyponatraemia** — NORMAL serum osmolality; check lipids, protein
- **Hypovolaemic hyponatraemia** (e.g. diarrhoea/vomiting, diuretics, Addison’s) — low volume + urine Na <20 (non-renal) vs >20 (renal)
- **Euvolaemic hyponatraemia** (SIADH, hypothyroidism, glucocorticoid deficiency, polydipsia) — normal volume + urine osmolality >100 (SIADH) / <100 (polydipsia)
- **Hypervolaemic** (heart failure, cirrhosis, CKD) — oedema + raised JVP + low urine Na (<20)
- **Splitter:** **Urine osmolality** <100 mOsm/kg → primary polydipsia or beer potomania (low solute intake); >100 mOsm/kg → SIADH or hypovolaemic/hypervolaemic (but need volume status + urine Na)

### Investigations
- **Bedside (first hour):**
  - Assess volume status (orthostatics if safe)
  - Urine dipstick (protein? glucose? to suggest CKD/diabetes)
  - **Capillary refill, JVP**
  - If severe symptoms: **3% NaCl ready** — do not delay bloods
- **Bloods:**
  - **U&E**, glucose (correct Na for hyperglycaemia: Na + 1.6 per 5 mmol/L glucose >5)
  - **Serum osmolality** (essential — distinguishes true from pseudohyponatraemia)
  - **Cortisol (random)** If suspected adrenal insufficiency: cortisol <100 nmol/L strongly suggestive; start hydrocortisone empirically while waiting.
  - **TSH, free T4** (hypothyroidism)
  - **Lipid profile** (pseudohyponatraemia from hypertriglyceridaemia)
  - **Uric acid** (low in SIADH, high in hypovolaemia)
- **Imaging:**  
  - First-line: **CXR** (if suspect SIADH/chest pathology e.g. small cell lung cancer)
  - Second-line: CT head (if neurological signs refractory to correction)
- **Special tests:**
  - **Urine osmolality + urine Na** (paired with serum) — key to classification.
  - **Short synacthen test** (if random cortisol borderline 100–400 nmol/L)
- **Diagnostic criteria:**  
  - **SIADH criteria** (Bartter & Schwartz):
    - Low plasma osmolality (<275 mOsm/kg)
    - Inappropriately concentrated urine (urine osmolality >100)
    - High urine Na (>20–40 mmol/L, with normal salt/water intake)
    - Clinical euvolaemia
    - No renal/thyroid/adrenal/glucocorticoid deficiency
- **Never-miss test:** **Random cortisol** (or 09:00 cortisol) to screen for adrenal insufficiency — omission can lead to fatal shock if steroids not given.

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - **Severe symptoms** (seizures/coma/decreased GCS):
    - Start **3% sodium chloride** (do not wait for full labs) — typical: 150 mL IV over 15–20 min ([check local protocol]).
    - Aim for **5 mmol/L rise in 1 hour** (or until symptoms resolve).
  - **Hypotensive / adrenal crisis suspected**:
    - Give **hydrocortisone 100 mg IV stat** (after blood for cortisol).
    - 0.9% saline 500 mL bolus (if hypovolaemic) — but avoid in SIADH.
  - **Mild–moderate** (Na >120, no severe symptoms):
    - Stop any offending drugs (thiazides, SSRIs, carbamazepine, etc.)
    - Identify volume status → guide fluid strategy.
- **Ward (first 24–72 hours):**
  - **Hypovolaemic**: replace with **0.9% saline** (1–2 L/day depending on deficit); monitor Na q6–8h.
  - **Euvolaemic (SIADH)**:
    - **Fluid restrict** to 800–1000 mL/day (or less if Na <125).
    - Avoid 0.9% saline (will worsen hyponatraemia).
  - **Hypervolaemic**: treat underlying (e.g. heart failure — diuretics, no further saline).
  - **Monitor Na** every 6–8 hours (more frequently if correcting rapidly).
  - **Target correction rate**:
    - Chronic hyponatraemia (>48h duration or unknown): **≤10 mmol/L in 24 hours** (≤8 in high-risk groups — alcoholism, malnourished, liver disease).
    - Acute (<48h): can correct more aggressively (up to 1–2 mmol/L/h) to prevent cerebral oedema.
  - **If overcorrection** (rise >10 mmol/L in 24h):
    - Stop hypertonic saline, give **desmopressin 1–2 µg IV/SC** to re-lower Na, and/or give D5% IV (1000 mL over 3–4 h) — discuss with senior.
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Address cause (stop causative drug, treat SIADH with fluid restriction ± urea 15–30 g daily if NICE-converted, or tolvaptan under specialist guidance — [check local policy]).
  - **Adrenal insufficiency**: lifelong hydrocortisone + fludrocortisone.
  - **Hypothyroidism**: levothyroxine.
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - **Fluids**: either restricted (SIADH) or 0.9% saline (hypovolaemic) — *never* 0.9% saline in euvolaemic SIADH.
  - **VTE prophylaxis**: LMWH unless contraindicated.
  - **Diet**: NBM only if starting 3% NaCl (airway risk); otherwise normal diet.
  - **Observations**: NEWS2 + strict **intake/output chart** + daily weight.
  - **Daily U&Es**.

### Drugs & Doses
- **3% sodium chloride** (hypertonic saline):  
  - 150 mL over 15–20 min if severe symptoms — [check local protocol for exact rate].
- **Hydrocortisone** (adrenal crisis):  
  - 100 mg IV stat, then 50–100 mg IV q6h (or 200 mg/24h infusion).
  - *Check BNF or local guideline.*
- **Desmopressin** (DDAVP) for overcorrection:  
  - 1–2 µg IV/SC once — [check local policy].
- **Fluid restriction**: 800–1000 mL total/day for SIADH (adjust based on Na).
- **Key contraindications / interactions**:  
  - No thiazide diuretics in hyponatraemia (avoid).
  - NSAIDs worsen SIADH.
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment**:  
  - Tolvaptan contraindicated in liver disease (risk of hepatotoxicity).  
  - Fluid restriction in CKD may cause AKI — monitor creatinine.
- **Most dangerous dose error**:  
  - **Giving 0.9% saline to a euvolemic SIADH patient** → worsens hyponatraemia (water retention > Na gain).  
  - **Correcting chronic hyponatraemia too fast** (>10 mmol/L in 24h) → osmotic demyelination syndrome (ODS) — catastrophic.

### Complications
- **Acute**:  
  - **Cerebral oedema** (if Na <120 rapid) → herniation, death.  
  - **Seizures** (from low Na).  
  - **Overcorrection** → **osmotic demyelination syndrome** (delayed 3–7 days): dysarthria, dysphagia, quadriparesis, locked-in syndrome.  
  - **Adrenal crisis** (if missed cause).
- **Chronic**:  
  - Increased falls/fractures (mild chronic hyponatraemia impairs gait).  
  - Osteoporosis (SIADH-related).  
  - Persistent symptoms (fatigue, cognitive impairment).

### Prognosis
- Overall mortality depends on underlying cause (sepsis > SIADH > polydipsia).  
- Severe hyponatraemia (Na <120) with correct management: ~10% in-hospital mortality.  
- ODS is permanent if not recognised early.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Always measure paired serum + urine osmolality and urine Na** before starting treatment — avoids incorrect classification.  
- **If severe symptoms, start 3% NaCl immediately** — do not wait for osmolality (draw blood simultaneously).  
- **Stop all non-essential fluids** — often the cause is iatrogenic (e.g. 5% dextrose post-op).

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Do not give 0.9% saline for SIADH** — it's the wrong fluid (water retention > Na gain).  
- **Do not give D5% for hyponatraemia** — this is water, not Na.  
- **Do not correct chronic hyponatraemia too fast** — limit to ≤8 mmol/L in 24h if high risk (alcoholics, malnourished).  
- **Do not forget to check drug chart** — thiazides, SSRIs, carbamazepine, PPIs common culprits.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:**  
  - Worsening headache, confusion, vomiting, seizure.  
  - Inability to tolerate oral intake (risk of recurrence).  
  - Lower limb weakness or difficulty speaking (suggesting ODS — urgent re-admission).
- **Follow-up:**  
  - Repeat U&E in 1 week (or sooner if on new drugs).  
  - If SIADH: clinic review (renal/endocrine) to arrange long-term strategy.

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior (Reg/Consultant) immediately if:**  
  - Na <120 mmol/L (especially if symptomatic).  
  - Seizures / GCS <13.  
  - ODS suspected (new dysarthria/dysphagia after correction).  
  - Overcorrection >10 mmol/L in 24 h.  
  - Haemodynamic instability (likely adrenal crisis — need ITU/HDU).  
- **ICU referral:**  
  - Persistent seizures despite 3% NaCl.  
  - Need for continuous monitoring of Na correction (e.g. hourly).  
  - Adrenal crisis requiring inotropes.  
  - NEWS2 ≥7 or lactate >4 mmol/L.
